text:
# Europe wants to join the AI race

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html)

27/05/2025 • 4 minutes reading

[Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/julia-krauwer/index.html)

Europe wants to get a grip on artificial intelligence, but is not yet a serious opponent in the global AI race. Most computing power, investments and leading companies are in the United States or China. Yet initiatives are also emerging in Europe to regain ground, with their own infrastructure, investments and AI startups that excel in niches.

European AI startups raise much less capital than their American competitors. In 2024, around $90 billion went to American AI startups and scale-ups, according to data from Dealroom. In Europe, that amount remained at less than 16 billion. According to Maarten Cleeren of Techleap, this is problematic. “In ten years, there won't be a company that doesn't use AI. If we don't put money into the tech companies that support this, we'll have a problem.”

## Huge investments

For the time being, European AI startups are fishing in a much smaller pond in terms of financing and are less likely to grow into heavyweights. The investments that such AI heavyweights in turn make are enormous. The American OpenAI, known for the smart chatbot ChatGPT, joined forces with fellow countryman Oracle and the Japanese SoftBank under the name Stargate at the beginning of this year. In this joint venture, the technology companies will invest at least $500 billion in AI infrastructure in the coming years.

The fact that China's share in global AI investments is relatively low is mainly because innovation there is largely supported by large technology companies such as Baidu, Tencent and Alibaba, which work closely with the government. Due to limited transparency on the capital market, many investments remain out of the picture.

However, the results of China's efforts are receiving increasing attention. For example, the Chinese startup Butterfly Effect recently launched the AI ​​agent [Manus](https://www.businessinsider.com/manus-ai-china-agent-hype-deepseek-2025-3?international=true&r=US&IR=T#:~:text=,others%20are%20calling%20it%20overhyped), which carries out complex tasks from start to finish with minimal human input. The DeepSeek AI model was surprising at the end of 2024 because it was developed with much less computing power, but achieved results comparable to the well-known language models from OpenAI and Meta, among others. This makes the AI ​​race not just a competition between American tech giants, but rather a global race with the United States and China at the center. Europe has not yet managed to gain a serious position in this race.

## Criticism of AI legislation

The European Commission's AI Act, adopted in March 2024, is the world's first comprehensive legislative package for artificial intelligence. The regulation imposes strict requirements on so-called high-risk applications and is encountering resistance in parts of the tech sector. “Potentially disastrous,” trade association EU Tech Chamber called the AI ​​Act; the rules would impose “strict obligations” on innovation [suffocate](https://www.euronews.com/next/2023/12/15/potentially-disastrous-for-innovation-tech-sector-says-eu-ai-ac t-goes-too-far#:~:text=%27Potentially%20disastrous%27%3A%20Tech%20sector%20says,that%20underpin%20many%20downstream).

Some AI entrepreneurs fear high compliance costs and legal uncertainty. “Founders don't know exactly where the boundaries are,” says Cleeren of Techleap. “Companies will be reluctant to bring certain AI features to the European market that are being launched in the United States.”

## Investing in AI factories

Yet Europe is now making an attempt to gain ground. In February 2025, the European Commission launched the InvestAI initiative, intended to attract 200 billion euros in investments in artificial intelligence. On April 10, the AI ​​Continent Plan was presented, which includes thirteen 'AI factories' and even five 'giga factories'. These facilities make high-quality infrastructure available for training large models.

There is also plenty of initiative from technology companies. The Iliad Group, a French tech and telecom conglomerate, announced in February an investment of 3 billion euros in sovereign AI infrastructure. It invests in the large data centers of subsidiary OpCore and serves the market with AI computing power through cloud company Scaleway. Mistral, a French company that develops advanced AI models itself, is one of Scaleway's customers. France wants to become a major hub for artificial intelligence.

## Position within the value chain

An analysis by consultant McKinsey shows that Europe within [several links](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/mckinsey/business%20functions/quantumblack/our%20insights/time%20to%20place%20our%20bets%20europes%20ai%20opportunity/time-to-place-our-bets-europes-ai-opportunity.pdf) of the AI value chain has acquired an important place. This applies, for example, to the machines used to make advanced chips, in which the Eindhoven-based ASML has a virtual monopoly. The IT service providers, including Capgemini, Ordina and Tietoevry, that help companies embed AI in their processes, also have a relatively large market share. Although Europe is lagging behind in the development of the so-called foundation models - the AI ​​models that are trained on enormous amounts of data and can produce new content based on it - Europe's market share is growing, according to the consultant. The same applies to the applications that are developed based on these models.

In the Netherlands, Cleeren from Techleap also sees how AI companies excel in specific areas and therefore acquire an important position within the value chain. “Weaviate uses AI to find and understand information based on what you mean, not just what you type,” he says. “And Deeploy is a platform that helps companies with a responsible implementation of AI.”

Europe participates in the development of foundation models, but the largest players are in the US and China. European potential now lies mainly in specialized applications, responsible use of AI and a growing network of startups and infrastructure projects. A foundation is being laid with initiatives such as INvestAI and the emergence of companies such as Mistral, although the road to a level playing field remains long.

## Read more in the technology sector

Technology, Media & Telecom (TMT) is one of the fastest growing sectors. Aided by the increasing use of software, algorithms and sensors, this sector is the basis for the digital innovation of other sectors.
